And you're not going after easy [ __ ] by any means, which I also admire about you. It goes back to the impact over happiness thing. Yeah. And so we're talking about this and I'm like, were you—because usually true interest is revealed early. So if we go and look at your life story, people ask, "What hobbies do you have? " I'm like, "I like to read. " Yeah. And like what else?

I'm like, "Yeah, I like to read. " That's it.

I was like that when I was five. I wasn't like, at five years old, "What's a good hobby for my future self-development? " It's like, "No, I just like reading. I like books better than people in general. " Um, and so you see that in your early life. Like you're starting companies and you didn't even know it was a company—it's just trying to do something.

So I was like, "Oh, you must have been interested in investing forever. " And you're like, "No, I never even thought about it until 2018. "

Then you said the funniest thing. You go, "How did you learn about—" He goes, "I started listening to Patrick's podcast and then I'd hear an idea like, 'that's a good idea, I would try that. ' Oh, I don't like that idea. " And then I start reading books, and then you're just literally taught yourself.

You weren't interested at all and now you're essentially world-class at it in a very short amount of time. And this is just the face again. You just did the same face.

But like, explain how you think about this, where it's like I always say learning is not memorizing information. Learning is changing your behavior. Mh. And I think if I thought of anybody else I know that personifies that, it's you. It's like, I'm not just listening to this podcast for shits and giggles. Yeah. I'm not reading this book even if you find it enjoyable. Yeah.

Like I'm looking to apply this.

Well, I I I I think it's back to that, like, um, sort of these two concepts we talked about. Um, my whole journey is one of self-mastery and finding out how I can become the best version of myself. Um, and meanwhile what gives me energy is solving problems. Um, you know, and as you say, I think this is revealed early.

I loved— I didn't know they were companies but I loved sorting out problems for other people.

I've been doing it my entire life, whether it's relationship advice or all these other things. And this is why I think these conversations are important to have, because you're the personality type that I've read about. It's just like now instead of reading about you when you're 80 or dead, you're in the middle of it.

So it's fascinating to me because I'm trying to balance my understanding from reading with an actual person.

Most people aren't like, "I'm starting a company for company's sake. " They're just like what you said: I like solving problems. Yeah. That's all. So if you look at the video games I actually think what kind of video games you like is a pretty good revealer too. I wasn't playing any kind of first-person shooter games much.

Most of what I played were strategy games—Civilization, business strategy games—I played every tycoon game about how to run businesses better.

Those were sort of my favorite games growing up and I loved SimCity. SimCity was amazing. Uh, this is what I would say about entrepreneurs: it's the best job in the world. I don't understand the people that give it up. I hate even saying this because rule number two in the center family, right, is what I teach my kids: mind your own business.

So I don't have any suggestions on how you should live your life.

If we're friends we can talk and go back and forth, but I just mind my own business. Yeah. But sometimes I— I meet so many entrepreneurs and they're like, "I sold my company. " I'm like, "Sorry to hear that. " They're like, "What? " I'm like, "Why would you get out of the game? " And then they sell their company and they go be a VC. I'm like, "Oh my god this is the best game in the world.

It's never ending. " It's not like sports where you get worse with time; you can solve problems, build your own world.

If you think about what we were doing in the computer with SimCity, it's like, "Oh I'll put a highway there." That's like—I'm the world builder. You get to control who's around you, what the rules in the world are, what happens, what's the outcome for the people in the world. And you get to make it—and hopefully you're doing it for benevolent and good reasons.

One of my favorite quotes from the history of entrepreneurship comes from Henry Ford: money comes naturally as a result of service. The crazy thing about Henry Ford is by 1919 he owned 100% because he bought out his investors of one of the most valuable companies in the world. He didn't do it to start a company. He had one idea. I've read 10 books on this guy and people knew him. He had one idea.

I've read 10 books on this guy and people knew him forever. He's like, "One idea: it's kind of weird that we're putting together cars. They're really expensive." He was obsessed with machinery and essentially outsourcing human labor to machines—started on the farm, right? He just wanted to build a car for the everyman.

You said something very in the early days of Spotify—he's like, "I have this goal of, you know, this celestial jukebox or whatever the case is. " He said at the very beginning, "I don't know how I'm going to do it. " He learned how to do it. Henry Ford was like, "How can I make a car cheaper? Sure as hell I can't put it in by hand.

We've got to learn how to mass-produce something we don't know how to do yet. " That took him a decade and a half of failure or whatever.

He didn't start the company to make money, but he made millions of people's lives better. He changed the geography of the world for God's sake. And as a result, money came naturally as a result of service. That's 100% how I think about it. I think this is the beauty of capitalism, right?

Ultimately, there has to be someone willing to pay for what you're doing, and the reason they pay is you're solving a problem for them.

The better you're solving that problem, or the bigger the problem you're solving for people, the more valuable it becomes. What did you say earlier too? You're like—I forgot how you phrased it—but in my mind when I interpret what you said it's this idea of not being a go-getter but being a go-giver. You say something like, the more problems you solve the more comes back to you. Was that the—

Well, so these are two different ideas but sort of closely related. It was actually my co-founder who said the value of a company is the sum of all problems solved. If you really think about it, it is exactly what it is. I tell this to the team because when we face very difficult problems, it is great because if we solve these problems we will create a lot of value.

I keep saying you think like Bezos. There's a great line—same thing—where I read his shareholder letters for the fourth time and they're so good and so clear and concise, I should read them every year. I felt like I just read them and I looked it up—I hadn't read them since the end of 2022.

But there are stories in Bezos's early career where people in Amazon would come to him with a huge problem, and he got excited. He'd be like, "Oh, great—like we had a problem we didn't even know. If we solve this, our company gets even more valuable. "

There's this guy named Henry Kaiser who was as famous in his day as maybe like an Elon is today. He built a hundred companies; one of them still exists, Kaiser Permanente. He built the Hoover Dam, he built the Liberty ships in World War II. He has this great maxim: people would come to him depressed and he'd be excited. They'd say, "What the hell's going on here, Henry?

" He goes, "Problems are just opportunities and workloads. "

If I can solve this problem for other people, I make their lives better and I make the company more valuable. And you also have to feel good about what you're creating in the world, 100%. But I think it comes from solving problems. I get excited—today you sort of asked about the other stuff I get focused on. It's not so much about the solution; I focus on the problem.

I want to find really interesting problems and figure out, even if there's like a five or ten percent chance of solving that problem, and I know that would be huge for humanity or society if we figure it out, then it's amazing. That gets me fired up.

The intricacies of solving that problem are often very, very complex because it's not like other people don't know the problem exists—there are probably a lot of people who would agree that if we could figure it out, that would be really valuable.

I find this all the time, and you said it yourself: sometimes they're really small problems, sometimes they're gigantic and earth-shatteringly different. It's everything around life extension to just walking into a store and seeing things you don't like in the store. Those are problems. If you could do those things better, you could probably build a business.

The biggest thing is people have a misconception about what innovation is. They somehow think they need to figure out something entirely new. But the history of the world is we build on other people's ideas. An innovation is actually taking two or more things that were already well-known and putting them together in a new way.

It's really what it is. And so for me the most interesting thing—it's like laying a puzzle or anything else: I get to sit around and, um, because of meeting so many billion people, I get to listen to problems all around me all the time.

I try to, um, distill and figure out, okay, well this person said this thing, but what if you actually articulate the problem like this instead? Okay, well what does that mean and what does that unlock? And that, for me, is just, um, it's so much fun and I couldn't imagine, you know, not spending every single day doing this.

You know, we, we, we started this journey by telling my story that I didn't have to work and that's why I started Spotify — because I love music and I wanted to figure out a way where consumers got what they wanted and creators were able to get paid by doing what they love to do. Um, and that's really the genesis of the story.

But even today, you know, I'm thinking about this and I said: if even if you remove all the money — even from the beginning, even in the middle, and even now — there's no way I wouldn't do this and spend so much of my awake time thinking about this stuff. Um, just sort of like for me this is impact and this is what leads to

happiness. In my life story I read something Jeff Bezos said that changed my perspective on the importance of high-quality sleep. He said that he makes sure he gets eight hours of sleep a night and as a result his mood, his energy and his decision-making is improved. His point was: you get paid to make high-quality decisions and you can't do that if you're sleeping terribly.

And the product that has made the biggest impact on my quality of sleep for years is Eight Sleep. I'm lucky enough to be friends with the founder of Eight, Matteo, and we live in the same city. A few months after I started using Eight Sleep, I randomly ran into Matteo at a restaurant and I was with some friends.

So I go over and say, "Hi." When I got back to my table, my friend asked me, "Who were you talking to?" And I said, "That's Matteo, the founder of Eight." And my friend replied, "He looks like he gets good sleep." Matteo is living and breathing his product.

I had never had the ability to change the temperature of my bed before I had an Eight. I had no idea how much that would improve the quality of my sleep. My Eight is ice cold. It's cold before I get into bed, so I fall asleep faster and wake up less during the night. That feature alone is worth ten times the price.

There are very few no-brainer investments in life and I believe Eight is one of them. That is why elite founders like Mark Zuckerberg and Elon Musk have all said publicly that they use Eight. I would recommend getting the Pod 5, which is the newest generation of their signature product.

It is a smart mattress cover that you place on top of your existing mattress and it is next-level sleep tech. It automatically regulates your body temperature throughout the night independently for each side of the bed. The result is you get up to a full hour of additional quality sleep per night.

Make the no-brainer investment in your sleep by going to eightsleep.com/enra and use the code senra to get $350 off. You can try it for 30 days at home and return it if you don't like it, but I'm confident you will love it. I will never let anyone take my Eight from me. Make sure you get yours at eightsleep.com.

You said something in the early days of Spotify that was very fascinating: you wanted to build an enduring and impactful company that did something that no one else did, right?

It's remarkable also, like when I'm listening to this series that was created in 2021, how much you guys were talking about AI and how early you were and you understood the impact — you kind of saw clearly where we're going.

But you said something that I didn't actually understand because the way I think about technology is like: technology is just a better way to do something, right? And you're like, at Spotify though, at the very beginning, I think you nailed it. You're like: the decisions that were made have to do with the DNA of the founder because we're the only ones there.

But you said, "I like technology for technology's sake. " Yeah. What does that mean?

Um, I like the process of science. I like the process of discovery. I like the process of understanding how things work. Um, you know, I've been wired this way all my life. I've been pulling apart computers. I've been trying to understand why a semiconductor works the way it works. All these things.

Um, and I'm intrigued about that — just this thing about understanding everything: understanding life, understand where we come from, understanding all these different things.

But I'm equally interested in solving problems. And when you're curious about both of those angles, you can end up finding these sort of, um, connection points where those two things meet — where it's obvious that there is something over there that no one's really applied in this way before and maybe there's not one idea but two ideas over there. It's quite interesting.

And I think you have to love technology in order to go to the depth of understanding what's possible, because oftentimes the greatest ideas come from truly innately understanding something and truly innately breaking constraints around that something — by understanding the rules and knowing when you can, yeah, break them.

So what are the greatest entrepreneurs really? Well, the greatest founders are the people that kind of have this one idea — they can almost internalize the consumer. Right? Like: what is someone willing — what do they need even if they can't articulate it themselves?

They have this entire field of amazing, brilliant engineers and scientists and mathematicians and all these groups of people doing various things and then sort out the intersection.

And then you actually have to make it viable too, which means you have to have some sort of business model because if you don't have that eventually this thing won't be sustainable. So, you know, it's sort of the trifecta of those three things.

That makes it even more interesting because now we're talking about a really complex equation trying to get these things together with multiple unknowns, and you're trying to configure these things by locking down constraints on one side and then trying another side.

The amazing thing about early-stage entrepreneurship is — we talked about this Google example of trying different colors — you can't do that early on.

Every decision you make is life or death. That makes the stakes even higher because you may literally try one thing and then run out of money. So you have to make sure that is the right thing. Um, it's such a fascinating process, but it comes back: it's a process of creativity, it's a process of trying things out.

And I'm more and more enamored and more and more in love with this idea that creativity itself is this really, really powerful thing. It may be the thing that makes us unique as humans relative to everything else that exists in this world, and I'm kind of going deeper and deeper into creativity itself.

It doesn't conform, it doesn't scale, and it doesn't behave like other things. And then you have this other side which is all about scale — which is about conforming and navigating that dynamism between these two polarities. Very few people, I think, can do that and are really, really classically good at doing it.

I think most of us can't even describe why we're doing what we're doing at the time. And what I mean by that is: you hinted on this earlier — it's so easy to kill new ideas, right? The arrow on the side of "no, we can't do this.

" It's amazing to me how many times this comes up in all these biographies where it's like this is a skill you have to learn, and a lot of it is being comfortable with the messiness of the creative product process.

But it's also that entrepreneurs are by definition impatient, patient. Yeah. They have this thing that looks like a paradox where they want stuff done now. I love what Jeff Bezos says about this: "step by step, ferociously." You know, that's the — I'm willing to plan, you know, to take two decades or three decades to solve the problem that I'm trying to go after.

Maybe in some cases stuff that he's working on might even outlive his lifetime, but it doesn't mean I'm dilly-dallying on a day-to-day basis. I heard you say something one time — you're like, "I promise you I don't think I'm the smartest. I don't think I'm the most talented. If I have one superpower, it's like I just have super — I have super patience." Yeah.

Um, I think Neco is an example of this. I met your co-founder on it and I got to see this too and I was like, "Oh, like this is cool. Who made the machines? " It's just like, we did. Yeah. And then I didn't know, like I don't know if "stealth" is the right word, but from the idea to the first time the customer happened, it was what, like six years or something or four years?

No, the company's eight years old and we barely began.

Yeah. And it's like, okay, that's an insane level. That's an understanding — like we're tackling something that's—yeah. I like what you just said: innovation and creativity is actually a combination of things that occurred before that we're combining.

When you mentioned that I thought of, you know, James Dyson — one of my entrepreneur heroes — and what I love about one of the hidden things people don't know is he wrote a book called The History of Great Inventions. It's kind of a bland read, God bless his heart, but it's just him going through little encyclopedia-style pages about what each inventor did, how he did it, and what the result was.

Then you see him take ideas from like 1700 or whatever and think, "Oh, I'll just take that little piece out here and combine it with this piece," and then ask, "What's the new technology today that enables me to solve this problem that didn't exist a few years ago?" So you have this patience and this desire to solve problems.

It sounded like to me — and maybe you could disagree — like you see a giant puzzle that you're trying to figure out. Now you have more resources, you can be patient; you couldn't have done Neco 20 when you were 23. Right. But you have this great quote that I know is important to you: "Quality is never an accident. It is always the result of intelligent effort."

We haven't talked about quality at all, even though Spotify is high quality — if you compare it to your competitors it's just in a class by itself. So it's obviously very important to you. We have to talk about quality. Do you want to pick up where that quote leaves off?

Well, there are so many strands of quality, right? There's the distinction of taste — whether taste is subjective or objective. You can go so many ways with this question, but the way I think about quality is that so much of your early life — certainly in my case — was the formulation of just trying to go for more.

And the further I've gotten, uh, I've realized, um, and and it's about everything, right? You amass more stuff. uh, you mess, uh, you try to do, um, you know, more things at the same time. You you try to do all of these things, um, at the same time.

And the further you go, you realize that, um, it it it really is about sort of ultimately this very simplistic tribe thing: less is more. And and the older I get, I feel is more. And and the older I get, I feel it more and more. Um, you know, we talked about it with friends.

Um, I think most people, uh, start out thinking having more friends is better, but I think more people are happier with fewer but better friends than many friends. Um, and and so I think quality is ultimately this sort of notion around, um, focusing, um, distilling, uh, getting to the essence.

Uh, we we can even talk about like quality in communication is often trimming things down and saying less things, right? things, right? Um, why is it, for instance, that when we're talking about very hard things, most people's instant reaction is just to try to add more complexity to the issue instead of just simplifying it.

They're just trying to, um, be courageous and just trying to say the thing right out loud. The older I get, the more my instinct is towards turning towards that. And I'm reminded even if — towards that. And I'm reminded even if we go back to investing because, like, part of why I sort of liked investing has nothing to do with money.

It started with, uh, honestly this fact that all of a sudden I had more money than I knew what to do with and I sort of didn't want to just hand it off to a bank and, and, uh, without understanding anything about it. But what sort of got me deeper curious about it is, uh, I realized that investing is is actually more about, um, learning

about your temperament than it is about the specific action that you're doing. Your temperament actually it's it's more about being in line with your temperament than than not, or which game you're picking needs to be suited to you and your circumstances and how you want to play this than anything else.

And so again we come back to philosophy. So, uh, one of the concepts, um, uh, is obviously around, um — that Munger talks about, coming back to Munger reference again — is it's diversification, right? And and he calls it somewhat simplified diversification because the common wisdom, of course, is in theory diversification is the best thing you can do.

But the truly greatest people, um, financially — if that's the only yardstick you use — typically do completely the opposite of that, which is they have, oftentimes, only one asset. Maybe they'll have two or three, but they certainly don't have more than that and they just go for that.

There's a realization you have, right? 'Cause like I read something you read a line and you're like, okay let me filter that through like how I would say it. So I I do this all the time where, like, I'll take notes on all the books that I read and then I'll reread them. I'll just jot it down. It's like what's occurring there; can I put it into one sentence?

And one thing I stumbled upon about this was that sometimes the, uh, Nick Sleep has this great quote where he's like, "The best investors are not investors at all. They're entrepreneurs who never sold." And me and you have talked about this, the fact that there's a lot of people that have like sold way too early and they're like lauded.

And it's like what if you just didn't do that? Everything else you did — like, if you just didn't sell. So Nick Sleep talks about this, and in other books it's like if Sam Walton didn't give away the stock in Walmart to his kids before it appreciated, you know, you have one of the largest fortunes by a single person ever for the ownership, the the concentration of ownership in Walmart.

Sam talks about — he's like, I didn't really do investing in anything but Walmart, right? And it's like he wasn't waking up saying I need to diversify my portfolio, I need to worry about capital allocation. He's just like I believe in what I'm doing; I'm going to keep doing it. I'm going to throw all my money in there and he's just like I doing because I love to do it, and spending time doing

anything else would be an absolute distraction. And Nick Sleep talks about this because he's a capital allocator. He's not building a product, but he's making capital allocation decisions like, "Do I need to sell this stock, put it into another one? I'm weighing my opportunity costs." And he was haunted almost by this idea.

If you look at financial history, it's like he talked about a firm, a financial firm one day where they're like, "Okay, uh, we owned IBM in the 1950s." And I didn't know till I read Michael Dell's autobiography, right? Because IBM was — it's like — they're the most powerful and wealthiest company in the world when me and you were born. Okay.

And Michael Dell's like, "You have to understand, like, 1987 for me to like yell out to my dad." He's like, "What do you want to do for a living?" He's like, "I want to compete with IBM. I'm a 19-year-old in my dorm room with $1,000. Like, what kind of grandio ambition do you have?" I went, like, why is this so crazy?

It was the most valuable company in the world by market cap at the time. It was the first company to go over a hundred billion dollars in market cap, right? And this little 19-year-old kid's like, "No, I think I can have a go at them." But Nick Sleep talks about he came into this firm and he's reading their — their — how they talk about their investments and like, "We made a massive mistake."

"We owned IBM in the 1950s and we sold." And they're having this conversation in the 1970s and the leadership of the financial firm is like how do we avoid not doing this? They said if we just held that it would have, uh, the gain would have been more than all the assets we have in our management. Like, we can't do this.

That same year they sold Walmart. They had Walmart in the 1970s. So they made the same mistake. And Buffett, obviously greatest investor of all time, he met Walt Disney. He went and talked to Walt Disney in like the 60s and then he — I think he bought the stock at like 62 cents or something, right? And he he had like a 2x or 4x game. He's like I sold this thing. Yeah.

So it's that's why I was like, "Oh, the best financial decisions are not financial decisions at all. You're doing it for another reason," which is exactly what you said. It's like all Sam had to do was just keep doing Walmart. That's exactly what he did.

I remember, um, I obviously live in Miami, uh, on a friend's boat in, uh, the marina there and there's like the boat like bigger than anything else around there.

It's like and you can Google like who owns it. It was like it was Sam Walton's brother's daughter. Right. Right. Yeah. It's crazy. But but but I think you're on to something important, um, which is this notion around just, um, innately focusing, um, and and solving problems day by day builds quality, right? Um, and and and so, uh, quality for me is is less.

Quality for me is, uh, focus. Quality for me is improving day by day. Um, all of these things build quality and quality is rare. Um, quality in people is rare, quality in ideas is rare, and we tend to make these things binary. Um, and, u, we talked about it with people. Like I'd rather have that person that has one good idea in, um, in an entire hour and the rest is crap than someone who has,

you know, 10 decent ideas, but nothing is amazing. So that sort of differential equation between the 1% versus the rest is is so important and it's so important to understand, and I think it's it's — we again take it literally, uh, and we know about it because we can explain it in hindsight but we don't know when it's happening.

So what is that qualitative process? What defines quality as it's happening before you can see it objectively from the outside? Those things are interesting and, um, I'm more and more drawn towards that.

Um, it is, um, for me at least very much looking at the people in it and looking at how they build judgment over time, the sort of feedback loop they create, the curiosity that they have about this and the obsession they have about trying to achieve the impossible.

And for me, the impossible is something that's perfect. It's never going to exist. The whole universe is this thing where the only thing we've learned, we keep debating all these things about the universe, but the only thing we know is it's not static. It moves. It cannot by definition be like this one thing. It's just constantly expanding or contracting or it's moving.

So perfection just doesn't exist. But the aspiration towards perfection, uh, is a remarkable thing. I mean, I love Japan for this reason, right? Like you you find these amazing individuals that literally spend their entire life.

um, in Japan — maybe 10 days ago or something — and I I I was in one of these temples, u, with a tea master who literally all he's done, uh, for the past — uh, I've asked him — for the past 34 years is perfecting how to make tea. That's it. Nothing else.

Uh, and yes, the tea is — it. Nothing else. Uh, and yes, the tea is amazing, but it's not just that. It's just seeing that obsession about quality, seeing that obsession about being not even 1% but like .1% in something. And I think feel like you you sort of asked about AI and all these things and and we can talk about art too, but like, uh, for me like

that is going to be even rarer. Like average is going to be possible to do even better than average is going to be possible to do with AI. But but that sort of thing about this guy doing this for 30 years just becoming the very, very best about what they do or even what I find incredibly inspiring about you is just the relentlessness every single day towards the long term.

It's it's like as you say, it's the paradox about the long-term, um, um, mindedness, uh, but the obsession on the daily basis, u, and against conventional odds, against all these other things. Because I I I would imagine — I don't know, but I would imagine like when you started, uh, founders probably not many people knew who you were. Nobody, nobody, right?

And so the competition for your time — uh — was there was none. So you could probably sit for 6 hours or 8 hours, etc. But today it's much harder. Um, because there's many other things. There's people like myself and other people and you're like, "Oh, wait a minute.

Maybe this is a good idea to meet this person or there's a, uh, you know, a business opportunity that shows up and maybe people are asking look maybe you should invest and maybe you're tempted about it even, but it's

this is the thing that happens all the time and this is how greatness gets evaporated: you lose focus. That's a great line — greatness gets evaporated. A lot of the stuff like when I talk on the podcast or everything I say on social media is like reminders to myself because I just read reread past highlights 'cause like we forget that, forget, and so I need a reminder.

And I just came across one. It's just like, uh, I've had them presented to me in a random order. So it's like I never know what I'm going to read that day and it said, uh, it was Buffett. It's like the difference between successful people and and really successful people is really successful people say no to everything. Yeah.

And this is exactly the constant thing where like I do have a question for you. Um, one going back to that — it's like I can't even explain why I did what I did because like I had close to no traction for five and a half years. Mhm. And now I look back like there's no way I would do that today. Yeah. But somehow that person — yeah — who I now greatly appreciate a lot that he did.

I feels like a different person. Yeah.

Context continued: person Yeah. who I now greatly appreciate a lot that he did. I feel like a different person. Yeah.

I cannot believe that — that is what I'm most proud of. It's not like trying to make something that's great, which I think is a selfish thing for us. Yes, when you release it to the world, you release on Spotify, you do all these things. It's an act of service to other people, but you're also doing it because you have to — it has to come out. Yeah.

But I just cannot believe that I did that. And I'm just like that's the thing I'm most proud of more than anything else. I had to do this. I was willing to like drive Uber if I had to, 10 bar, do literally whatever. I was like, just give me enough time, I'll figure it out now. I didn't think it was going to take half a decade to figure out.

You have a ton of stories with Spotify with that. There is no line, I feel, between your work and your life. Like they're one and the same. The issue that I'm having now in general is that I feel a lot of pressure because I'm trying to change not just my life but literally the trajectory of my entire family.

I feel very guilty when I don't work to the point where, you know, I just got back from a crazy trip that people would love to go on and I just felt guilty for taking time off and not working.

I feel like this is almost like a compulsion — I don't have control over it. Yeah. Do you have anything like that? Sure. Um I do feel the same thing, of course. Um but I'm back to sort of time versus energy management and I don't know about you, but I feel like the greatest ideas I've had come from the most weird and wonderful places where I expected nothing out of it.

Quite often when I do take some time off, I come back with two or three entirely new insights that just wouldn't have come if I kept grinding in that thing. Just changing the scenery, being in a different frame of mind, pausing, giving, you know — it's in the creative process. The greatest artists talk about some of the greatest songs literally taking five minutes to write. Yeah.

Which is amazing, right?

But some songs are the ones you work on; you put it in a drawer and six months later, totally unbeknownst to you, it clicks. You're not even working on that thing. It just sort of like, oh, that's it, and they go back and they do the song and it's like the greatest song ever. There is not one path to greatness. There's many. And I feel like that's why I'm so obsessively focused on energy.

So, you mean energy is like you just let that guide you? Yeah. It's like, hey, if I need a break right now, if I need to go for a walk or if I need to spend time with my wife and kids, or like — yeah. Is that what you mean? Yeah. 100%. And I try to feel that. I feel like so much of our life rips us apart from this thing and tries to get us on a schedule, per se.

It's not like I don't have a schedule — of course I do — but so much of it should be guided by trying to understand ourselves more intimately.

I don't know if you've heard of this, but going back to sleep, there has been this kind of thing more recently, which I was surprised my 10-year-old daughter told me about. She sort of said like we have this idea of eight hours, and she actually mentioned instead that the real notion is that we kind of did it almost like fasting.

Like Ramadan is based on the sun — sometimes it could be six hours, sometimes it could be 12 hours — so it used to be the sleep was actually in two periods.

You didn't sleep one consecutive thing; you sort of had a three- or four-hour sleep, you woke up, and then you had another three- or four-hour sleep again. So much of that was based on light and maybe driven by other things happening in our life too.

For Nordic what it actually meant, going back as late as the 18th century before we had electric lights and candles, is we actually slept a lot less in the summers and a lot more in the winters, guided by light.

So we keep thinking it's a static thing but it's actually driven by the environment around us. So much of this innate knowledge about listening to ourselves, understanding our innate personality, understanding hunger — like I can tell you, as someone, I've gained in periods of my life like 40 lbs in my worst periods, etc.

One of the problems I have now is that I literally don't know when I'm hungry because I ruined that natural feeling in my body of understanding when I'm hungry and when I'm not.

A huge part of losing weight for me over the past few years was really starting to listen to my body again and starting to figure out what satiation means because for me, for instance, I don't feel it until 20 minutes after. So if I didn't sort of eye what I should eat, I would just keep eating way more than I should.

So much of me has been portion sizing and understanding, okay, well that's probably going to be enough. It doesn't feel like enough at that moment because I ruined my body, but 20 minutes later I understand it.

I'm just trying to convey this about understanding who you are, choosing the game you're playing, and realizing that life is not one game but a thousand games. There's this brilliant quote by this guy called Wame Opia — it's another one of those things I just circled. Perfect timing. We're — No, we're in perfect alignment. Go ahead. Well, I'm probably going to ruin the exact one.

So maybe you can read it. Okay, I'll read it. It's: "In life, the challenge is not so much to figure out how best to play the game. The challenge is to figure out what game you're playing. "

For me, realizing that has been eye-opening. It's another one that's on my wall because quite often when I'm talking to people and they're asking for life advice, they're not playing their game — they're playing someone else's game.

They're certainly not playing the game they want to be playing, but they somehow think life is just one game when actually so much is about choosing the right game for you.

I keep coming back to that: energy management is the same thing. You have to create the environment around you that you want to do. You have to choose your game. When you do that and understand it, you start becoming superhuman in your ability to get things done. Do you have more or less negative self-talk today than you did 20 years ago?

I — I am more comfortable with who I am than I was 20 years ago.

I've been a searcher my whole life. In my teens I went to every possible religious meetup you could — I went to Hare Krishna centers, Jewish centers, mosques, everything — and I tried to learn as much as possible because I truly believe not enough people are interested in where we come from and what the purpose of life is.

Those are some of the greatest questions and I, like many others, don't have any idea, obviously, what will happen, but I think they're really important.

So I've always been a searcher and that led me to search about myself too, trying to find who I am. The older I've gotten and the more things have started clicking about myself, the more unapologetic I am about who I am, and so your inner monologue gets less harsh.

Because not only do you stand out quite a lot as an entrepreneur and my interests were widely different than my social circuit growing up, but the second thing is I'm an introvert. That doesn't help.

Most people get energy from being around a lot of people. I didn't, so that naturally gets you to question yourself. As an entrepreneur, I'm not the most eloquent person, I'm not the smartest — you're pretty damn eloquent. Well, I had to work on it. That's the hard thing.

I've really listened to all your episodes and all your interviews; I did this before we were friends, and every time something comes out I was like, "Oh, I got to watch that. " You're very — you're not giving yourself enough credit. You are a very clear communicator.

Thank you. But it's really a work product. I wish there was more recorded stuff of me when I was 20 and 21 because I was not great. I was quite terrible.

So I've learned that communication is a superpower and something I have to work on — getting my message across to other people so they believe what I'm believing and see what I'm seeing, in order to get them to want to come and join whatever thing we're trying to will into this world.

I have two more questions that I'm personally curious about. One is going to be a very weird question, but the first one is: do you feel different? Most people watching or listening know so few people are born in the projects in a tiny little — we didn't even — we'll have to do this every year if you're fine with it.

Because the idea that you come from this tiny little island which I got to visit and then you compete with the biggest companies in the world and you win — it's just, like, we didn't even get there.

You grow up, you have a great mom, single family, not a lot of money, and now your lived experience is so rare that few people can empathize because they also had it. So I'm very curious: do you feel different?

As you've grown from a kid in the projects, even with early success, to now being one of the most successful entrepreneurs on the planet — which I know, don't do the face, but it's an undeniable fact — do you feel different?

I think yes and no. I feel different in the same way a 40-year-old would feel different than a 10-year-old. I'm a product of all the experiences of my life and I've been incredibly fortunate. I was counting the other day — I've been to like 130 countries or something in my life. I've seen so many cultures, so many people. I have friends all over the world. All of that has shaped me into who I am.

I don't feel different because many of the things that drive me — the long-termness, the obsession, this willingness on the paradox of winning on the one hand but also trying to truly find a win-win and work with people to find a win-win — have always been there. You look back at yourself a few years back and say, "Wow, how did I do this? " and you're amazed at that.

I can feel the same way about young Daniel. young Daniel.

Um and and you're amazed at that. I can feel the same way about Young Daniel. Young Daniel.

I can feel the same way about the young Daniel who worked every weekend, um um, you know, non-stop 24/7; sacrificed so many things — so many summers, so many other things, um you know.

I had a blast doing so, but I didn't have a normal upbringing, uh, just because I was so obsessed about learning, so obsessed about making it, um and and it put me in the position of where I am today.

Um, and I actually feel like I owe that guy to keep pushing myself, right? Because there's so many things, there's so many demands of my time, so many things that tell me to, to sort of downshift gear, uh into an easier gear because life would be a lot more comfortable that way. This is not a comfortable thing.

Impact to happiness. This is what— I think I texted this to you, um, but one one of the roles you play, and I'm very grateful that you allowed us to like record this conversation, is that essentially what I text you was: you seem to have like no self-imposed ceiling on what you can learn or achieve.

And that spending time with you then transfers that belief to other people. So, like, every single time, you know, obviously, like, I take notes of, like, what I learned from the conversation we have after the fact and I interpret it — it's like obviously not verbatim, I'm saying, like, what did I just learn from Daniel, what does it apply to me?

He's like, "Oh, you have all these." So, you think you don't. I thought I was ambitious and I, like, I didn't even realize till you kind of pulled the scales off my eyes, like, you have this like limiting belief that you have no limits. There is no limits in life. And so like you're a hell of inspiring.

One other question for you. Um I'm obsessed with Game of Thrones to an unhealthy degree. Um I rewatch the series all the time. I've read all the books. I read the encyclopedia. I'll read the family histories because I do think, like, you can learn a lot from human nature in fiction. And my job and my obsession requires me to — 99% of my reading is non-fiction, and I don't...

I want to read more fiction.

I came across something recently because I'm going through Jeff Bezos shareholders again and then I read like what was my interpretation of this in the past and then I came across something that was fascinating.

There's a there's a story in the prequels of Game of Thrones where, uh, two brothers, um, are fighting in this war.

One of the brothers was pulled into the war that did not want to be there and he made the sacrifice because it was for the good of his family. He wound up dying in the war that he didn't want to fight in in the first place, and his brother survived.

The brother got his remains, buried him, and then put him in a grave with only one word on his tombstone and that one word was "loyal."

So hopefully you don't—you, 100 years from now when you do have a tombstone, if there was only going to be one word on that tombstone, what would you want it to be?

Um, I, I don't think too much anymore to think a lot about how other people saw me or see me. Um, I don't do that anymore. Um, so I would, I would choose more of a self-reflective, uh, one. Um um um, and I wish, um, only one thing, um, on my tombstone — future one. It feels absurd talking about it, but, uh, would be that um he lived.

That's a great one. That's a great way to end it. Daniel, I'm thankful for your time. Thankful for your friendship. You're one of the people I most admire and uh I really appreciate you doing this.

Well, well, thank you so much and it's such a huge honor to be your first guest in this series.

Yeah, of course. Thank you so much. Wouldn't have happened either way. I hope you enjoyed this episode. Please remember to subscribe wherever you're listening and leave a review. And make sure you listen to my other podcast, Founders.

For almost a decade, I've obsessively read over 400 biographies of history's greatest entrepreneurs searching for ideas that you can use in your work. Most of the guests you hear on this show first found me through Founders.
